alps.s: "Heidi" is a delightful story for children of life in the Alps, one of many tales written by the Swiss authoress, Johanna Spyri, who died in her home at Zurich in 1891.

child.n: She had been well known to the younger readers of her own country since 1880, when she published her story, Heimathlos, which ran into three or more editions, and which, like her other books, as she states on the title page, was written for those who love children, as well as for the youngsters themselves.
child.s: "Heidi" is a delightful story for children of life in the Alps, one of many tales written by the Swiss authoress, Johanna Spyri, who died in her home at Zurich in 1891.

child.n: The record of the early life of this Swiss child amid the beauties of her passionately loved mountain-home and during her exile in the great town has been for many years a favorite book of younger readers in Germany and America.

draw.s: Quickly the winter passed, and still more quickly the bright glad summer, and now another winter was drawing to its close.

god.n: If we ask Him for something that is not good for us, He does not give it, but something better still, if only we will continue to pray earnestly and do not run away and lose our trust in Him.
god.s: God is a good father to us all, and knows better than we do what is good for us.

god.n: And then this one gets into trouble, and cries, 'Save me, God, for there is none other to help me,' and God says, 'Why did you go from Me; I could not help you when you ran away.'

god.n: So will you not go and ask Him to forgive you, and continue to pray and to trust Him, for you may be sure that He will make everything right and happy for you, and then you will be glad and lighthearted again."

gold.s: There was a reddish gold light over the higher peaks; a light breeze springing up and the branches of the fir trees moved gently to and fro the sun was on its way.

gold.n: The two grey mountains peaks lifted their lofty heads against the sky and looked solemnly down upon the valley as of old; the great bird was poised aloft in the clear blue air, and the mountain wind came over the heights and blew refreshingly around the children as they sat on the sunlit slope.

grow.n: "But the child is not a goat or a bird, she is a human being.
grow.s: "I am going to let her grow up and be happy among the goats and birds; with them she is safe, and will learn nothing evil."

"
grow.s: Clara had grown much more cheerful; she no longer found time hang heavy during the lesson hours, for Heidi was continually making a diversion of some kind or other.

grow.n: Fraulein Rottenmeier was always very much annoyed that the old lady continually addressed her by her surname only; but it was no use minding, for the grandmother always went her own way, and so there was no help for it.
grow.s: "My worthy Rottenmeier," replied Frau Sesemann, "if a person is called 'Heidi' and has grown accustomed to that name, I call her by the same, and so let it be."

grow.n: How are you getting on in your school-time; do you like your lessons, and have you learnt a great deal?"
grow.s: When at last she saw that Heidi was growing calmer, she said, "Now I want you to tell me something.

grow.n: She lay awake for long hours at night, for as soon as she was alone and everything was still around her, the picture of the mountain with its sunshine and flowers rose vividly before her eyes; and when at last she fell asleep it was to dream of the rocks and the snow-field turning crimson in the evening light, and waking in the morning she would think herself back at the hut and prepare to run joyfully out into--the sun--and then-- there was her large bed, and here she was in Frankfurt far, far away from home.
grow.s: But the weight of trouble on the little heart grew heavier and heavier; she could no longer eat her food, and every day she grew a little paler.

grow.n: But as matters did not mend, and she saw that many mornings Heidi had evidently been crying before she came downstairs, she took her again into her room one day, and drawing the child to her said, "Now tell me, Heidi, what is the matter; are you in trouble?"
grow.s: She let some days go by to see if the child grew brighter and lost her down-cast appearance.

grow.n: She entered into the lives of all the people she read about so that they became like dear friends to her, and it delighted her more and more to be with them.
grow.s: And then grandmother liked to hear her read aloud, and the oftener Heidi read her tales the fonder she grew of them.

grow.n: She promised to write at once to her father, and to have her bed put in her room and not to be left alone for a moment.
grow.s: Clara, in short, grew very excited, and Fraulein Rottenmeier had great difficulty in quieting her.

grow.n: He took her hand and said in a kind, soothing voice, "There, now you feel better, and now tell me where you were wanting to go to?"
grow.s: Then he sat down beside her and waited until Heidi had grown quieter and no longer trembled so violently.

grow.n: Presently she pushed her book gently in front of him and said, "See how happy he is there," and she pointed with her finger to the figure of the returned prodigal, who was standing by his father clad in fresh raiment as one of his own sons again.
grow.s: "You are right, Heidi; it is a beautiful tale," he replied, but he looked so grave as he said it that Heidi grew silent herself and sat looking quietly at her pictures.

growl.n: Then Heidi flung herself against him and cried indignantly, "You have no right to touch her, it will hurt her, let her alone!"

grumble.n: He began whistling and said no more, while Heidi looked around her and began to tremble with excitement, for she knew every tree along the way, and there overhead were the high jagged peaks of the mountain looking down on her like old friends.

grumble.s: "Oh, I don't care about that," he grumbled in reply.

guess.n: "Many unlikely things happen in life," said Frau Sesemann with a pleased smile. "
guess.s: And it is almost as astonishing to me that you should have guessed such an unlikely thing."

guest.n: Heidi had been sent up to her room and ordered to remain there until called down, as the grandmother would certainly like to see Clara alone first.

guest.n: Then the two men sat down in front of the hut, and Heidi had her little place too, for the doctor beckoned her to come and sit beside him.
guest.s: The old man held out his hand to his guest in friendly greeting.

guest.n: As the doctor shook hands with her she asked, "Would you like to come out with the goats to-morrow morning?"
guest.s: Arrived at Peter's home Heidi bid the others good-bye; she was to wait at grandmother's till her grandfather, who was going on to Dorfli with his guest, returned to fetch her.

guest.n: There was no constraint about the meeting, for they both knew each other perfectly well from hearsay and felt like old acquaintances.
guest.s: Grandmamma having also reached the top, dismounted, and gave Heidi an affectionate greeting, before turning to the grandfather, who had meanwhile come up to welcome his guests.

guide.n: When Heidi was lying that night on her high bed of hay she thought of the grandmother on her low pillow, and of all she had said about the light and comfort that awoke in her when she heard the hymns, and she thought: if I could read to her every day, then I should go on making her better.
guide.s: Peter got his sleigh, put Heidi at the back, he himself sitting in front to guide, and down the mountain they shot like two birds darting through the air.

guide.n: "It's not saying good-bye, grandmamma," Clara called out, "for you will come up now and then and see how we are getting on, and we shall so look forward to your visits, shan't we, Heidi?"
guide.s: The bearers and the horse and guide were now seen approaching; the former were sent back at once, and grandmamma prepared to mount for her return journey.

gust.n: It came howling and wailing down the chimney like voices of those in pain, and it raged with such fury among the old fir trees that here and there a branch was snapped and fell.
gust.s: The wind grew so tempestuous during the night, and blew in such gusts against the walls, that the hut trembled and the old beams groaned and creaked.

gust.s: Then came a thick warm shawl for the grandmother, in which she could wrap herself well up and not feel the cold when the wind came sweeping in such terrible gusts round the house.

habitable.n: Otherwise the wind and rain and snow blew into the rooms, so that it was impossible even to keep a candle alight, and the indwellers would have been frozen to death during the long cold winters.

hair.n: Heidi meanwhile had not been idle; she had made the round of the room and looked carefully at everything there was to be seen.

hair.n: The doctor had had an only daughter, who, after his wife's death, had been his sole and constant companion, but only a few months previously death had deprived him of his dear child, and he had never been the same bright and cheery man since.
hair.s: There was an expression of sadness on his face, formerly so cheerful, and his hair had grown greyer since the spring.

hair.n: He reached home in this disturbed state of mind.

hair.s: One a tall girl with fair hair and pink cheeks, leaning on Heidi, whose dark eyes were dancing with joy.

half.n: "The child has young blood in her veins and a good roof over her head, and let me further tell the pastor, that I know where wood is to be found, and when is the proper time to fetch it; the pastor can go and look inside my wood-shed; the fire is never out in my hut the whole winter through.
half.s: I cannot think but what you must be half-frozen to death in this hut in the winter, and I do not know how the child lives through it!"

half.n: The door was shut close to prevent the light being seen in the hall outside, which might frighten away the ghost.

Fraulein Rottenmeier looked at her clock: it was just half-past four; she had never got up so early before in her life.

half.n: After a while sleep fell on Heidi too, and after her disturbed night and early rising she slept so soundly that she did not wake till Sebastian shook her by the arm and called to her, "Wake up, wake up!
half.s: "Yes, yes," said Sebastian, half asleep; "she is sure to be alive, there is no reason why she should be dead."

half.n: A distinguished soldier had lived there at one time; he had taken service in Spain and had there performed many brave deeds and gathered much treasure.

half.n: "That is just what I wish most myself, Clara," replied her father, whose face grew happier each time he looked at his little daughter. "
half.s: And I keep on thinking what I could do for him, or what present I could make him that would give him half as much pleasure as he has given me."

hand.n: Then she drew a deep breath--in the eagerness of her thirst she had not stopped to breathe--and put down the bowl.
hand.s: Heidi lifted the bowl with both hands and drank without pause till it was empty, for the thirst of all her long hot journey had returned upon her.

hand.n: Just at this moment the pleading young cry was heard again, and Heidi jumped up running and, putting her arms round the little creature's neck, asked in a sympathetic voice, "What is it, little Snowflake?
hand.s: Then there was little White Snowflake, who bleated in such a plaintive and beseeching manner that Heidi already had several times run to it and taken its head in her hands to comfort it.

hand.s: The young animal turned quickly, and began contentedly eating the leaves out of Heidi's hand.

hand.s: Dete had now got the bundle under her arm and the child by the hand, and so they went down the mountain together.

handsome.n: Clara's little face was thin and pale, and at this moment her two soft blue eyes were fixed on the clock, which seemed to her to go very slowly this day, and with a slight accent of impatience, which was very rare with her, she asked, "Isn't it time yet, Fraulein Rottenmeier?"
handsome.s: A handsome bookcase with glass doors explained why it was called the study, and here evidently the little girl was accustomed to have her lessons.

hang.n: "It's the doctor--my old friend the doctor," exclaimed Clara and her father at the same moment, and Herr Sesemann smiled to himself at the thought of what his friend's opinion must have been of this new way of satisfying his thirst for water.
hang.s: "He was kind and laughed, and he had a thick gold chain and a gold thing hanging from it with a large red stone, and a horse's head at the top of his stick."

hang.n: The great snow-field sparkled in the bright sunlight, and the two grey rocky peaks rose in their ancient majesty against the dark blue sky.

hans.n: In all her stories she shows an underlying desire to preserve children alike from misunderstanding and the mistaken kindness that frequently hinder the happiness and natural development of their lives and characters.
hans.s: Madame Spyri, like Hans Andersen, had by temperament a peculiar skill in writing the simple histories of an innocent world.

happen.s: It happens well, however, for us, for he can now see after the child, and you can the better tell me your tale."

happen.n: Now Dete was not quite easy in her own conscience about what she was doing, and consequently was feeling hot and irritable, and said more than she had intended.

happen.s: So she drew the little stool out from under the table, put it in the corner and sat down upon it, and there silently awaited what would happen next.

happen.n: "That's all right then--and here comes your little friend.

happen.n: Two things coming together may produce a happy result, as for instance, a fresh zeal for learning and a new method of teaching, and neither does any harm.

heidi.n: She had been well known to the younger readers of her own country since 1880, when she published her story, Heimathlos, which ran into three or more editions, and which, like her other books, as she states on the title page, was written for those who love children, as well as for the youngsters themselves.
heidi.s: "Heidi" is a delightful story for children of life in the Alps, one of many tales written by the Swiss authoress, Johanna Spyri, who died in her home at Zurich in 1891.

heidi.n: The record of the early life of this Swiss child amid the beauties of her passionately loved mountain-home and during her exile in the great town has been for many years a favorite book of younger readers in Germany and America.
heidi.s: Her own sympathy with the instincts and longings of the child's heart is shown in her picture of Heidi.

instinct.n: The record of the early life of this Swiss child amid the beauties of her passionately loved mountain-home and during her exile in the great town has been for many years a favorite book of younger readers in Germany and America.

preserve.n: The authoress, as we feel in reading her tales, lived among the scenes and people she describes, and the setting of her stories has the charm of the mountain scenery amid which she places her small actors.
preserve.s: In all her stories she shows an underlying desire to preserve children alike from misunderstanding and the mistaken kindness that frequently hinder the happiness and natural development of their lives and characters.

region.s: I mean one so at home among the living creatures of the high, pure mountain regions, that she would be like some idealistic being from another world among us."

regular.n: Grandmamma passed her hand carefully over it to make sure there were no bits of hay sticking out. "
regular.s: The two had meanwhile ascended to the hay-loft and begun to prepare a bed; there were so many articles piled one over the other that when finished it looked like a regular little fortress.

regularly.n: "She will do no such thing," said the old man with calm determination.
regularly.s: This is the last winter that she must be allowed to run wild; next winter she must come regularly to school every day."

regularly.n: Peter promised, for the trepidation he had been in had made him quite tame and docile.
regularly.s: But you must come regularly, not now and then as you do to school; even if it snows it won't hurt you."

reign.n: But soon another knock came to the door, and Sebastian again stepped in, this time to say that some one had brought a large basket with orders that it was to be given at once to Miss Clara.
reign.s: Quietness reigned again in the study, and lessons began once more; Fraulein Rottenmeier now took up her station in the study in order by her presence to prevent any further dreadful goings- on.

rejoice.n: After parting with the tutor she went down to the study to make sure of the good news.
rejoice.s: We can but rejoice that the child has made such a good start and hope for her future progress."

rejoice.n: You will not repent coming to live with us again; as for myself you will always be welcome as a dear friend and neighbor, and I look forward to our spending many a pleasant winter evening together, for I shall prize your companionship, and we will find some nice friends too for the little one."
rejoice.s: He pressed the old man's hand in his, and said with emotion, "Neighbor, you went into the right church before you came to mine; I am greatly rejoiced.

rejoice.n: But the doctor walked with his eyes fastened to the ground and did not once lift them to the blue sky above him.
rejoice.s: It was a sunny September morning, so full of light and sweetness that it seemed as if everybody must rejoice.

rejoice.n: Then she let the child go, and Heidi ran into the next room, and bid Peter come quickly, for it had now grown quite dark.
rejoice.s: But when you come and read those words to me, then I am comforted and my heart rejoices once more."

rejoice.n: Clara found the life more charming each day and could not say enough of the kindness and care which the grandfather lavished upon her, nor of Heidi's lively and amusing companionship, for the latter was more entertaining even than when in Frankfurt with her, and Clara's first thought when she woke each morning was, "Oh, how glad I am to be here still."
rejoice.s: Meanwhile grandmamma down at Ragatz was rejoicing at the excellent news of the invalid which reached her daily from the mountain.

rejoinder.n: While Heidi was pausing to consider what this might be there approached from above a whistling, calling, and other sounds which Heidi immediately recognised.
rejoinder.s: "Then we will invite her on the couch with the beautiful green turf feather-bed," was her grandfather's quiet rejoinder.

relation.n: If you could promise me that Heidi shall never have to go and earn her living among strangers, then you would richly reward me for all I have done for your child."
relation.s: I have nothing to leave the child when I die, and she has no relations, except one person who will always like to make what profit out of her she can.

relationship.n: So we called him Uncle, and as through my father we are also related to nearly every family in Dorfli, he became known all over the place as Uncle, and since he went to live on the mountain side he has gone everywhere by the name of Alm-Uncle."
relationship.s: We, however, did not refuse to acknowledge our relationship with him, my great-grandmother on my mother's side having been sister to his grandmother.

relative.n: So it was let to poor people, who paid but a small rent, and when any part of the building fell it was allowed to remain.
relative.s: When after many long years it seemed certain that he was dead, a distant relative took possession of the house, but it had already fallen into disrepair, and he had no wish to rebuild it.

release.n: In the course of the afternoon the remembrance of Heidi's appearance the day before, as she was starting out on her travels, suddenly returned to the lady, and she made up her mind that she would supplement the child's clothing with various garments from Clara's wardrobe, so as to give her a decent appearance when Herr Sesemann returned.
release.s: Fraulein Rottenmeier was considerably relieved by his words, and released the tutor to his work.

release.n: Heidi undid her presents one by one until they were all displayed; she could not speak the while for wonder and delight.
release.s: After he had released it from its heavy outer covering, "There, child," he said, "now you can go on unpacking your treasures yourself."

relief.n: Clara now began to ask her questions about her home, and Heidi was delighted to tell her all about the mountain and the goats, and the flowery meadows which were so dear to her.
relief.s: It was a great relief to Heidi to know that the windows could be opened and that one could look out, for she still felt as if she was shut up in prison.

relief.n: Herr Sesemann was only home for a short time; he left for Paris again before the fortnight was over, comforting Clara, who could not bear that he should go from her again so soon, with the prospect of her grandmother's arrival, which was to take place in a few days' time.
relief.s: "O yes, I know," replied Fraulein Rottenmeier, but there was no tone of relief in her voice as she thought of the coming help.

relieve.n: In the course of the afternoon the remembrance of Heidi's appearance the day before, as she was starting out on her travels, suddenly returned to the lady, and she made up her mind that she would supplement the child's clothing with various garments from Clara's wardrobe, so as to give her a decent appearance when Herr Sesemann returned.
relieve.s: Fraulein Rottenmeier was considerably relieved by his words, and released the tutor to his work.

relieve.n: He drew Heidi aside and gave her a thick rolled parcel, and a letter for her grandfather; the parcel, he told her, was a present from Herr Sesemann, and she must put it at the bottom of her basket under the rolls and be very careful not to lose it, as Herr Sesemann would be very vexed if she did, and never be the same to her again; so little miss was to think well of what he said.
relieve.s: Sebastian was greatly relieved at not having to do any mountain climbing.

reluctantly.n: With Alm-Uncle, have I not told you so already?"
reluctantly.s: and more and more reluctantly Dete made answer, "Up there with Alm-Uncle!" "

reluctantly.n: Well I will let her off if you will give me some more of your cheese to-morrow," he said, for he was determined to have something to make up to him for his fright.
reluctantly.s: Peter looked with surprise at the commanding little figure, whose dark eyes were flashing, and reluctantly he let his stick drop. "

reluctantly.n: The door had hardly shut behind him before the whole congregation now came forward to greet Alm-Uncle, every one striving to be the first to shake hands with him, and so many were held out that Alm-Uncle did not know with which to begin; and some said, "We are so pleased to see you among us again," and another, "I have long been wishing we could have a talk together again," and greetings of all kinds echoed from every side, and when Alm-Uncle told them he was thinking of returning to his old quarters in Dorfli for the winter, there was such a general chorus of pleasure that any one would have thought he was the most beloved person in all Dorfli, and that they had hardly known how to live without him.
reluctantly.s: He did not say good-bye to him till they were standing outside, so that all the people standing about saw him shake hands as if parting reluctantly from his best friend.

remain.n: I have, I think, done my duty by her for these four years, and now it is time for you to do yours."
remain.s: "The child is here to remain with you," Dete made answer. "

remain.n: So the time went on, while to Heidi, who had so often looked up from the valley at the mountains above, these seemed now to have faces, and to be looking down at her like old friends.
remain.s: She drank in the golden sunlight, the fresh air, the sweet smell of the flowers, and wished for nothing better than to remain there forever.

remain.n: Fraulein Rottenmeier rose slowly and went up to the little new companion for the daughter of the house, to see what she was like.
remain.s: Dete remained standing politely near the door, still holding Heidi tightly by the hand, for she did not know what the child might take it into her head to do amid these new surroundings.

remain.n: Clara had looked on during the interview without speaking; now she beckoned to Heidi and said, "Come here!"
remain.s: Heidi remained by the door where she had been standing since she first came in.

remain.n: Heidi looked wonderingly at him for a minute or two, and then said, "Am I to eat some of that too?"
remain.s: Mute and motionless he still remained standing beside Heidi; it was not his duty to speak, nor to move away until she had helped herself.

remain.n: When after a while Heidi saw that her efforts were fruitless, she gave up trying, and began to think whether she would not go out and round the house till she came to the grass, but then she remembered that the night before she had only seen stones in front of the house.
remain.s: But the windows remained immovable, try what Heidi would to open them, even endeavoring to push her little fingers under them to lift them up; but it was all no use.

remain.n: The cover of the basket was loose, and at this moment one, two, three, and then two more, and again more kittens came suddenly tumbling on to the floor and racing about the room in every direction, and with such indescribable rapidity that it seemed as if the whole room was full of them.
remain.s: "On some considerations I am for it, on others against it," he began in answer; "for it, on the ground that if your whole attention is directed to the basket--" but the speech remained unfinished.

remain.n: Heidi sat herself down in a corner and repeated her instructions over to herself.
remain.s: Heidi had been sent up to her room and ordered to remain there until called down, as the grandmother would certainly like to see Clara alone first.

remain.n: "Because I would a thousand times rather be with grandfather on the mountain than anywhere else in the world."
remain.s: "If they were willing to let you stay, why did you not remain where you were better off than at home?"

remain.n: Where are Clara and grandmother?"
remain.s: But Heidi still remained standing, looking down the path with a questioning gaze. "

remain.n: Overhead the great bird was flying round and round in wide circles, but to- day he made no sound; poised on his large wings he floated contentedly in the blue ether.
remain.s: A soft, light morning breeze blew deliciously across the mountain, gently stirring the bluebells that still remained of the summer's wealth of flowers, their slender heads nodding cheerfully in the sunshine.

remain.n: As the doctor turned for the last time and looked back at the waving Heidi and the sunny mountain, he said to himself, "It is good to be up there, good for body and soul, and a man might learn how to be happy once more."
remain.s: And so the doctor again bid her good-bye and started on his way, while Heidi remained looking after him and waving her hand as long as a speck of him could be seen.

remain.n: This had now gone on for many years.

remain.n: A little later Heidi said, "It is growing dark and I must go home; I am glad to think, that you are quite well again."
remain.s: The old woman did not answer, but she had heard Heidi's words, and the expression that had made the child think she was better remained on her face.

remain.n: But just imagine, Fraulein Rottenmeier refuses to come with us.
remain.s: Grandmamma is travelling with me and will remain with me; she also is delighted at the thought of paying you a visit.

remain.n: The grandfather had meanwhile drawn up the invalid chair and spread some of the wraps over it; he now went up to Clara.
remain.s: O grandmamma," she said, "I should like to remain here for ever."

remain.n: You have brought up all kinds of shawls and covers with you, and we could make up a soft bed out of them, and as to the general looking after the child, you need have no fear, for I will see to that."
remain.s: I have been thinking," he said to her, "that if you were willing to agree to it, your little granddaughter might remain up here, and I am sure she would grow stronger.

remain.n: He allowed Heidi to do what she liked with him, for he was reduced to such a state of low spirits and meekness that he was ready to give his help to Clara without murmur or resistance.
remain.s: Even though Heidi had promised not to do anything, there still remained the lively dread that his deed might be found out in some other way.

remain.n: Then a bright thought occurred to him; he would then be able to think over the matter between now and next year's fair.
remain.s: Peter remained pondering; he was trying to think which of these two desirable objects he should best like to have, and he found it difficult to decide.

remainder.n: Heidi tenderly stroked the two goats in turn, running first to one side of them and then the other, and jumping about in her glee at the pretty little animals. "
remainder.s: Peter disappeared with the remainder of his flock.

remainder.n: The next day, when the hour came for Clara and Heidi to be together, the latter walked in with her book and proposed that she should go on reading aloud every afternoon to Clara, if the latter liked it.
remainder.s: Then the house seemed so silent and empty that Heidi and Clara did not know what to do with themselves, and sat during the remainder of the day like two lost children.

remainder.n: Then at last the grandmother spoke, "Heidi, read me one of the hymns!
remainder.s: I can feel I can do nothing for the remainder of my life but thank the Father in Heaven for all the mercies he has shown us!"

remark.n: She notices all that is going on, as I have often had occasion to remark, and this will stand her in good stead some day, for the old man has nothing beyond his two goats and his hut."
remark.s: "Oh, as to the looking after," remarked Dete, "the boy need not put himself out about that; she is not by any means stupid for her five years, and knows how to use her eyes.
To all their questions and remarks she made answer as she passed "I can't stop now, as you see, I must make haste with the child as we have yet some way to go."

remark.n: But I must tell you, Dete, that I am astonished to see so young a child.
remark.s: "Well, that's a name that one can pronounce," remarked Fraulein Rottenmeier. "

remark.n: "Ah, Herr Sesemann, you know what I mean," continued Fraulein Rottenmeier. "
remark.s: "Still I think even a Swiss child would have to touch the earth if she wanted to go anywhere," remarked Herr Sesemann, "otherwise they would have been given wings instead of feet."

remark.n: "If you only knew of one thing she has done, if you only knew of the kind of people and animals she has brought into the house during your absence!
remark.s: I see nothing shocking in the child," remarked Herr Sesemann quietly.

remark.n: She was surprised at what she had been told about Heidi's incapacity for learning, and determined to find out more concerning this matter, not by inquiries from the tutor, however, although she esteemed him highly for his uprightness of character; she had always a friendly greeting for him, but always avoided being drawn into conversation with him, for she found his style of talk somewhat wearisome.
remark.s: Fraulein Rottenmeier was prepared with some further remarks, but the grandmother had turned away and gone quickly towards her own room.

remark.n: Frau Sesemann wrote that she did not feel inclined to take the journey again from Holstein to Frankfurt because Rottenmeier fancied she saw ghosts.
remark.s: She wrote off without delay to Frau Sesemann, but got no more satisfactory reply from that quarter, and some remarks in the letter she considered were quite offensive.

remark.n: "I assure you, Peter," replied Heidi, "grandfather knows all about them.
remark.s: Peter remarked scornfully, "I dare say, when no one knows even where such people live."

remark.n: "It's a good job that no one but the wind did it," said the baker again, "or he might smart for it!
remark.s: "Uncle said the wind might perhaps have done it," remarked one of the women, who could not sufficiently admire the red upholstery.

remarkable.n: So she invited him to her room, and as he entered she held out her hand in greeting, and pushing a chair towards him, "I am pleased to see you," she said, "pray sit down and tell me what brings you here; nothing bad, no complaints, I hope?"
remarkable.s: It was about a week after this that the tutor asked Frau Sesemann's permission for an interview with her, as he wished to inform her of a remarkable thing that had come to pass.

remarkable.n: But this was not the most terrible part of his sufferings at the moment, for far worse was the fear and horror that possessed him, feeling sure, as he did now, that the policeman had really come over for him from Frankfurt.
remarkable.s: Peter, meanwhile, with all his efforts, could not stop himself, but went rolling on, and still tumbling head over heels at intervals in a most remarkable manner.

remember.n: So he began, telling Heidi the name of each goat in turn as he pointed it out to her.
remember.s: Peter knew these by heart, for having very little else to carry in his head he had no difficulty in remembering them.
Fraulein Rottenmeier having at last come to the end of her sermonizing said, "Now remember what I have said, Adelaide!

remember.n: The day came for grandmother's departure--a sad one for Clara and Heidi.
remember.s: And Heidi ran away and prayed that she might always remember God, and that He would go on thinking about her.

remember.n: He wished her a happy journey, and Heidi thanked him for all his kindness, and added, "And please say good-bye to the doctor for me and give him many, many thanks."
remember.s: As she stood by the carriage door, Herr Sesemann gave her his hand and said he hoped she would remember him and Clara.

remember.n: "I see, I see," smiled the doctor, "one can find out a great many thinks by thinking.
remember.s: O yes, I remember; I happened to pass through the dining-room and caught little miss's name, and I put two and two together--and so I thought--"

remember.n: But it was not long before the fir trees began their old song; Heidi felt it in all her limbs, and again the desire to run outside was irresistible, and she was off to play and leap to the tune of the waving branches.
remember.s: Then she suddenly remembered that her stool was left standing in the middle of the floor and that the table had not been rubbed, and she jumped up and ran inside again.

remember.n: She lifted her eyes and saw the sad expression in his as he looked down at her; she had never seen him with that look on his face when she was in Frankfurt.
remember.s: Then Heidi suddenly remembered why she had run down there, and that the doctor had really come.

remember.n: And Heidi read the words over two or three times, for she also found pleasure in this assurance of all things being arranged for the best.
remember.s: Read the verse again, that we may remember it well and not forget it again."

remember.n: Peter was accustomed to give immediate obedience to Uncle, and he marched off with his goats, but with a turn of the head and roll of the eye that showed he had some thought in reserve.
remember.s: So now be off and remember what I say."

remember.n: Clara, however, could not understand it; the fact was that she had never in her life before spent a whole day in the open air, much less in such high, life-giving mountain air.
remember.s: "Yes, I know what it feels like," replied Heidi, who remembered the many days in Frankfurt when all her food used to seem to stick in her throat.

remember.n: Clara sat silent, overcome with the enchantment of all that her eye rested upon, and with the anticipation of all the happiness that was now before her.
remember.s: Then she suddenly remembered that Clara was cured; that was the crowning delight of all that made life so delightful in the midst of all this surrounding beauty.

remember.n: When God has shown us some special mercy we should think at once of those who are denied so many things.
remember.s: In the midst of our own happiness we forget too often that which we ought to remember before all things.

remember.n: She gave a nod of satisfaction as she saw that Uncle had already taken Clara up in his arms and was following her with sturdy strides.
remember.s: she asked, remembering all at once that the child could not yet take so long a walk.

remembrance.n: This was Heidi's great sorrow, which re-awoke each time she thought about the darkness.
remembrance.s: The shadow over the eyes brought to her remembrance the grandmother, who would never again be able to see the sunlight and the beauty up here.

remembrance.n: He felt as if he could not now enjoy it.
remembrance.s: His conscience smote him at the remembrance of how he had stood with his doubled fists behind the doctor, who was now giving up to him his whole good dinner.

remembrance.n: Now do you understand me, boy?"
remembrance.s: You shall choose what you would like to have as a remembrance from the Frankfurt visitors, and as a token that they will not think any more of the wrong thing you did.

remembrance.n: Herr Sesemann now beckoned to the children as it was time to be off.
remembrance.s: Oh, then I shall send a hundred pounds of salt from Frankfurt, for I want her to have something as a remembrance of me."

remind.s: But go on now with your dinner," said Peter, again reminding her it was time to eat.

remind.n: But see, there is a beautiful tale to the picture which I will tell you this evening.

remind.n: Both children said their prayers, and each thanked God in her own way for the blessing He had bestowed on Clara, who had for so long lain weak and ill.
remind.s: "Yes, Heidi, I am sure you are right, and I am glad you reminded me; I almost forgot my prayers for very joy."

reminder.n: Can you tell me anything that you have wished very much to have?
reminder.s: But I should like you to have something for a pleasant reminder of the visitors from Frankfurt.

remove.n: The letter was successful, and two days later Herr Sesemann stood at his front door and rang the bell in such a manner that everybody came rushing from all parts of the house and stood looking affrighted at everybody else, convinced that the ghost was impudently beginning its evil tricks in daylight.
remove.s: Epileptic fits and St. Vitus's dance often came on suddenly in cases like this, and Clara was liable to be attacked by either if the cause of the general alarm was not removed.

renew.s: He continued his climb with renewed courage, and at last, after a long and exhausting walk, he came in sight of his goal.

rent.n: Since then it had stood empty, for no one could stay in it who had not some idea of how to stop up the holes and gaps and make it habitable.
rent.s: As long ago as when his son Tobias was a child Alm-Uncle had rented the tumble- down old place.

rent.n: In the middle of October he and Heidi took up their residence there.
rent.s: As soon as he made up his mind to spend the winter in Dorfli, he rented the old place and worked during the autumn to get it sound and tight.

repast.n: "I never enjoyed anything as much as this.

repay.n: Speak, friend, and tell me what I can do?"
repay.s: I can never repay all you have done, but whatever is in my power to do is at your service.

repay.n: I thank you heartily for all you have said, but I have need of nothing; I have enough for myself and the child as long as I live.
repay.s: "Herr Sesemann," he replied in his dignified way, "believe me that I too have my share in the joy of your daughter's recovery, and my trouble is well repaid by it.

repeat.n: "I am glad I am not the child!"

repeat.n: Now Barbel had for long past been most anxious to ascertain particulars about Alm-Uncle, as she could not understand why he seemed to feel such hatred towards his fellow-creatures, and insisted on living all alone, or why people spoke about him half in whispers, as if afraid to say anything against him, and yet unwilling to take his Part.
repeat.s: "You are right, I did, but I am not going to repeat what I heard; if it should come to his ears I should get into trouble about it."

repeat.s: Heidi sat herself down in a corner and repeated her instructions over to herself.

repeat.n: It had no longer the old troubled expression, but was alight with peace and joy as if she were already looking with clear new eyes into the garden or Paradise.
repeat.s: And the old woman kept on repeating the glad words, while Heidi beamed with happiness, and she could not take her eyes away from the grandmother's face, which had never looked like that before.

repeat.n: "Of course I am, if the doctor is coming too," replied Heidi.
repeat.s: said Peter, repeating the words with which he daily greeted her, either in question or in summons.

repeat.n: And Heidi took equal pleasure in them, for the picture of the beautiful sunny day of her return home rose before her eyes, and she exclaimed joyfully, "Grandmother, I know exactly what it is like to go home."
repeat.s: the grandmother repeated them once or twice to herself, with a look of happy expectation on her face.
Peter went at his task again and repeated the three letters so many times and with such determination that she said at last,--

repeatedly.n: She would stand underneath them and look up, unable to tear herself away, looking and listening while they bowed and swayed and roared as the mighty wind rushed through them.
repeatedly.s: She would run away repeatedly from whatever she might be doing, to listen to them, for nothing seemed so strange and wonderful to her as the deep mysterious sound in the tops of the trees.

repentance.s: "I don't want any clothes," said the child, not showing any sign of repentance for her past deed.

repentantly.n: "That is right, dear child," and anxious to cheer her, added, "Don't be unhappy, for He will do everything you wish in good time."
repentantly.s: "I will go at once and ask God to forgive me, and I will never forget Him again," she replied repentantly.

reply.n: "Very well then, I will tell you--but just wait a moment," said Dete in a warning voice, and she looked back to make sure that the child was not near enough to hear all she was going to relate; but the child was nowhere to be seen, and must have turned aside from following her companions some time before, while these were too eagerly occupied with their conversation to notice it.
reply.s: replied Barbel, somewhat offended, "gossip has not reached such a dreadful pitch in Prattigau as all that, and I am also quite capable of holding my tongue when it is necessary."

reply.n: He was the elder of two brothers; the younger was a quiet, orderly man, but nothing would please the other but to play the grand gentleman and go driving about the country and mixing with bad company, strangers that nobody knew.
reply.s: I should think so indeed," replied Dete with animation; "he was owner once of one of the largest farms in Domleschg.

reply.n: Tobias was taught his trade in Mels, and when he had served his apprenticeship he came back to Dorfli and married my sister Adelaide.
reply.s: "Wait a moment, I am coming to that, but I cannot tell you everything at once," replied Dete. "

reply.n: And I will give you bread as well, a large piece like you had to-day; but then you must promise never to beat Greenfinch, or Snowflake, or any of the goats."
reply.s: "You shall have it all, to-morrow and every day, I do not want it," replied Heidi, giving ready consent to his demand. "

reply.n: "But will it be like that to-morrow for certain?"
reply.s: "It is like that most days," he replied.

reply.n: But before the dinner bowls had been cleared away another visitor arrived, and this time it was Cousin Dete.
reply.s: He did not speak again the whole of that day, and the following morning when Heidi again asked the same question, he replied, "We will see."

reply.n: "No, no, you must not stop to talk; it is already too late," said Dete, holding Heidi, who was struggling to get away, fast by the hand. "
reply.s: "I am only just going over to Frankfurt for a little visit with Dete," she replied; "but I must first run in to grandmother, she will be expecting me."

reply.n: "But the windows won't open," responded Heidi sadly.
reply.s: "You must open the window and look out," replied Clara amused.

reply.n: Sebastian and Tinette were now sent for to clear up the broken articles and put things in order again; the tutor said good- morning and left, as it was impossible to do any more lessons that day; there had been certainly no time for gaping this morning.
reply.s: "Yes," replied Heidi, "but I will certainly not move again," for now she understood that it was a rule to sit still while she was being taught.

reply.n: "Get along home with you and don't try this trick on me again, or you may not come off so easily a second time," and with that he turned and was about to shut the door.

show.n: The authoress, as we feel in reading her tales, lived among the scenes and people she describes, and the setting of her stories has the charm of the mountain scenery amid which she places her small actors.

spyri.s: "Heidi" is a delightful story for children of life in the Alps, one of many tales written by the Swiss authoress, Johanna Spyri, who died in her home at Zurich in 1891.

story.s: "Heidi" is a delightful story for children of life in the Alps, one of many tales written by the Swiss authoress, Johanna Spyri, who died in her home at Zurich in 1891.

story.n: The authoress, as we feel in reading her tales, lived among the scenes and people she describes, and the setting of her stories has the charm of the mountain scenery amid which she places her small actors.

time.s: And so the time passed happily on till evening.

time.n: When the goats had finished licking up the salt her grandfather told her to go and fetch her bowl and the bread.
time.s: Heidi's questions came tumbling out one after the other, so that her grandfather had only time to answer each of them with "Yes, yes."

time.n: But Peter knew how to make himself heard, for he shouted till the very rocks above echoed his voice, and at last Heidi appeared, and when she saw the inviting repast spread out upon the ground she went skipping round it for joy.
time.s: "Leave off jumping about, it is time for dinner," said Peter; "sit down now and begin."

time.n: Heidi now took up the bowl and drank her milk, and as soon as she had put it down empty Peter rose and filled it again for her.
time.s: But go on now with your dinner," said Peter, again reminding her it was time to eat.

time.n: Just at this moment the pleading young cry was heard again, and Heidi jumped up running and, putting her arms round the little creature's neck, asked in a sympathetic voice, "What is it, little Snowflake?
time.s: Then there was little White Snowflake, who bleated in such a plaintive and beseeching manner that Heidi already had several times run to it and taken its head in her hands to comfort it.

time.n: She had decided that by far the handsomest and best-behaved of the goats were undoubtedly the two belonging to her grandfather; they carried themselves with a certain air of distinction and generally went their own way, and as to the great Turk they treated him with indifference and contempt.
time.s: Peter now having finished his meal joined Heidi and the goats, Heidi having by this time found out a great many things about these.

time.s: Heidi was quite sure this was the grandmother, so she went up to the spinning-wheel and said, "Good- day, grandmother, I have come at last; did you think I was a long time coming?"

tiny.n: She opened another door, and now found herself in another small room, for the place was not a herdsman's hut like her grandfather's, with one large room on the ground floor and a hay-loft above, but a very old cottage, where everything was narrow and poor and shabby.

zurich.n: She had been well known to the younger readers of her own country since 1880, when she published her story, Heimathlos, which ran into three or more editions, and which, like her other books, as she states on the title page, was written for those who love children, as well as for the youngsters themselves.
zurich.s: "Heidi" is a delightful story for children of life in the Alps, one of many tales written by the Swiss authoress, Johanna Spyri, who died in her home at Zurich in 1891.
